However,  there are two key challenges in leveraging RL
for vulnerability discovery. First, it is hard to automatically
identify effective vulnerabilities that  require determination
of accident  responsibility. When an accident occurs,  it  is
essential to attribute the failure to the autonomous vehicle’s

Fig. 1:  Illustration of effective and diverse vulnerabilities.
An effective vulnerability is an accident  that  is caused by
the false decision-making of the tested policy. Diverse vul-
nerabilities refer to different accident types that are consistent
with human understanding and regulations.
(AV) decision-making or those of other traffic participants.
An effective vulnerability is one where the tested policy is
responsible for the accident, revealing a genuine deficiency in
its driving policy [7]–[9]. Accident responsibility, however,
is complex to assess, often requiring human experts with
domain knowledge in real-world settings [10],  [11]. Simi-
larly,  in simulation-based RL training, designing a suitable
reward function for responsibility attribution also demands
substantial domain expertise [12]–[14], posing a challenge
to the automated discovery of effective vulnerabilities.
The second challenge in RL-based vulnerability discovery
is ensuring diverse  failure cases.  Since a safety-critical
flaw can arise from various accident  types,  it  is crucial
to comprehensively explore the space of possible failures
[7],  [9],  [15]. Traditional RL methods, however,  tend to
converge on the most probable failure case,  leading to a
lack of diversity in discovered vulnerabilities [16]. Existing
approaches  to diversity-aware RL generally fall  into two
types. The first type is optimizing a general diversity metric
alongside task objectives. However, these methods often lead
to vulnerabilities with uninterpretable differences  that are
not consistent with human understanding and regulations.
The second type is incorporating heuristic-driven diversity
measures tailored to specific tasks, which rely heavily on
expert knowledge and struggle to generalize across different
scenarios. These limitations hinder  the interpretability and

A. Diverse Vulnerability Discovery
Adversarial RL has emerged as a popular approach to
purposely generating safety-critical scenarios to efficiently
find vulnerabilities where autonomous driving policies make
mistakes. Kuutti et al. [4] use Advantage Actor Critic (A2C)

served Markov Decision Process (POMDP). defined by a tu-
ple M = (N, S, A, O, P,R, γ), where N = {1, 2, · · · , N}
is the set of agents, S is the state space encompassing all
possible states of the N agents, A is the shared action space,
O denotes each agent’s observation space, P is the transition
probability function, R is the joint reward function with γ
is the reward discount factor. At each step, agent i receives

To automatically find effective and diverse vulnerabilities,
we propose the AED framework that consists of three com-
ponents including automatic reward design, diverse accident
generation, and preference-based effectiveness enhancement.
The overview of our framework is shown in Fig. 2. The
first automatic reward design component prompts an LLM
with the environment and task descriptions to automatically

generate reward function samples and select  the best one.
Then, to enhance the diversity of vulnerabilities, we propose
a diverse accident generation component  that prompts the
LLM to consider a broad range of accident types and trains
adversarial policies for different  types in parallel. Finally,
to enhance the effectiveness of each vulnerability, we use
preference-based learning to train a reward model that filters
ineffective accidents and other accident types. Combining the
three components lead to our framework that automatically
discovers effective and diverse vulnerabilities.
A. Automatic Reward Design
To enable automatic reward design for vulnerability dis-
covery, we extend the In-Context Preference Learning (ICPL)
framework [14] to leverage the code-synthesis and in-context
learning capabilities of LLMs. In our adaptation,  the LLM
generates task-specific reward functions based on environ-
ment descriptions and human preferences, guiding the dis-
covery of effective accidents in a self-improving manner.
The LLM first  receives an environment description and
a detailed task specification,  then generates K executable
reward functions. The environment  text outlines  the sce-
nario and relevant code-level details,  such as  the agent’s
observation/action space and key parameters or  functions
useful for reward construction. The specific details of the task
description are provided in Sec. IV-B. Each reward function
is used to train an agent,  resulting in K agents, which
are evaluated using human-written criteria for  identifying
effective accidents. To ensure comprehensive coverage, we
also generate accident videos for human evaluation. Based
on both metrics and visual inspection, evaluators select the
best and worst reward functions. The selected best function
typically yields high accident effectiveness across scenarios
and serves as a basis for further refinement.
B. Diverse Accident Generation
To enhance diversity in vulnerability discovery,  the ad-
versarial  policy should capture distinct  characteristics of
various weaknesses  in the tested policy. To achieve this,
we  introduce a Diverse Accident Generation component,
which prompts LLMs to generate a set of reward functions
that promote vulnerability diversity. By considering a wide
range of accident types, LLMs automatically design multiple
reward functions in parallel, each tailored to induce specific
failure cases in the tested policy.
The process begins with the LLM generating a task de-
scription for a given accident type based on the environment
specifications. This description includes a high-level sum-
mary of the targeted accident scenario, concrete examples,
justifications for attributing responsibility to the tested agent,
and well-defined criteria for categorizing scenarios within
this accident type. Using the component outlined in Sec. IV-
A, the LLM then autonomously generates reward functions
corresponding to different accident types in parallel.
Once the reward functions are generated,  they are inte-
grated into the training of adversarial policies, each opti-
mized for a specific vulnerability of the tested policy. These

adversarial policies are then used to collect trajectory data,
creating a structured dataset covering diverse failure cases.
This dataset serves as a foundation for further analysis and
improvement of the tested policy, ensuring a comprehensive
evaluation of its robustness across various accident scenarios.
C. Preference-Based Effectiveness Enhancement
Reward functions derived from Sec.  IV-B for a specific
accident type are often imperfect, sometimes capturing unin-
tended accident types or ineffective scenarios. For instance,
the reward function for Left Lane Change (Accident Type
1) may capture Right Lane Change  (Accident Type 2)
and Adversarial Vehicle cut  in mistakenly (Accident Type
3). To  improve  the effectiveness and distinguish diverse
accident types, we propose a preference-based effectiveness
enhancement component to refine the LLMs-driven reward
function. The key idea is to learn a new reward function rˆ that
prefers trajectories corresponding to the intended accident
types over other  types. Specifically, rˆ is trained to assign
higher cumulative rewards  to Type 1 than to those from
Type 2 or Type 3.
To construct  training data for preference  learning, we
leverage trajectory data generated in parallel  for different
accident  types using their  respective LLMs-driven reward
functions.

In this way,  the reward function rˆ learns to favor Type
1 accident trajectories while effectively distinguishing them
from other  types. We then incorporate rˆ and the original
LLMs-driven reward function into RL training. If rˆ identi-
fies trajectories as Type 2,  the training will be pernalized..

We evaluate AED in two driving environments—Highway
and Roundabout—using the Highway simulator  [52]. We
consider three types of tested driving policy: two Planning-
based methods, Value Iteration (VI) [53] and Robust Value
Iteration (RVI)  [54],  and one RL-based method, Dueling
Double Q-Network (D3QN) [55]. To account for the com-
plexity of traffic interactions, we conduct experiments with
two and three adversarial vehicle agents in each environment.
A.  Implementation Details
We consider three distinct types of vulnerabilities in our
experiments: Left Lane Change (Left), Right Lane Change
(Right), and Rear End (Rear) collision scenarios. These
categories correspond to the different failure-inducing situa-
tions: an accident caused during a left lane change maneuver,
a right  lane change maneuver, or a rear-end collision. To
ensure robustness, each adversarial training is repeated with
three different  random seeds. Each adversarial  policy  is
trained using the MAPPO algorithm [24] for 10 million steps.
We utilize GPT-4o for diverse accident generation.
We compare  the performance of our method with the
following baselines:
• VDARS [7]: A multi-agent RL framework that employs
handcrafted reward functions  to learn the adversarial
policy for vulnerability discovery.
• Parallel Eureka: An ablated version of our method
without the preference-based effectiveness enhancement
module. This can also be regarded as a parallel version
of Eureka for different vulnerability types.
B. Evaluation of Diversity
To evaluate the capability of AED in discovering diverse
vulnerability scenarios, we measure the number of distinct
vulnerability types identified across multiple training itera-
tions. For each traffic environment setting—defined by the
traffic environment (Highway and Roundabout),  the tested
policy (VI, RVI, and D3QN), and the number of adversarial
vehicle agents  (2 and 3)—we conduct  three  independent

ent environments, tested policies, and numbers of adversarial
er three training iterations, where the first discovered type is
relevant frequency among effective failures. AED consistently
training  iterations with different  random seeds.  In each
iteration, we collect all effective failure cases.  In the first
iteration, we record the most frequently occurring effective
vulnerability type and assign it a count of 1. In the second
and third iterations, we exclude types previously discovered
and select  the most  frequently occurring type among the
remaining types. Instead of simply incrementing the count by
one, we add the relative frequency of the newly discovered
type—its proportion among all  effective  failures  in  that
iteration—to the cumulative count. In this way, each iteration
contributes at most one type, and the final number reflects
both the counts and the prominence of distinct vulnerability
types discovered. For VDARS, each adversarial policy is
trained using the same handcrafter reward function across all
iterations. For AED, the adversarial policy in each iteration
is trained using one of the three different reward functions,
which are automatically generated by LLMs in parallel and
targeted at different failure types (i.e., Left, Right, and Rear).
As shown in Fig. 3, AED consistently discovers a greater
number of distinct vulnerability types compared to VDARS
across all tested scenarios. In both the Highway and Round-
about environments, AED reaches or closely approaches the
maximum of  three  types within three  training iterations,
regardless of the tested policy and the number of adversar-
ial vehicle agents. In contrast, VDARS typically discovers
only one or two types, even after multiple iterations. This
discrepancy highlights the limited exploration capability of
using a single handcrafted reward function in VDARS. On
the other hand, AED benefits from multiple LLMs-driven
reward functions that promote the exploration of different
failure scenarios, enabling the adversarial agents to expose a
broader range of policy weaknesses.
C. Evaluation of Effectiveness
To evaluate the effectiveness of our method, we measure
the effective vulnerability rate, which quantifies how fre-
quently an adversarial policy induces a specific type of effec-
tive failure in the tested driving policy, where responsibility
can be attributed to the tested policy. This metric represents
the percentage of  simulation runs where  the adversarial
policy successfully triggers such an effective failure.

TABLE I: Effective Vulnerability Rates across different envir
agents. AED consistently achieves the highest effective vulner
AED achieves  the highest  effective vulnerability rates
across a wide range of environments,  tested policies, and
numbers of adversarial vehicles, consistently outperforming
or matching the baseline VDARS. It successfully discovers
and exploits hidden weaknesses in the tested driving policies,
including failure modes that VDARS fails to uncover. For
instance, in the Highway environment with a VI-driven tested
policy and two adversarial vehicle agents, AED achieves a
100% effective failure rate for Left Lane Change, compared
to only 34.24% by VDARS. Similarly,  in the Roundabout
environment with a DQM-driven tested policy and two adver-
sarial vehicle agents, AED improves the effective Rear-End
failure rate from 5.26% to 21.67%. These results demonstrate
that AED reliably increases  the effectiveness of  induced
failures across different traffic scenarios.
Ablation study: effect of preference-based enhancement:
To assess the impact of the preference-based enhancement
module, we compare AED with an ablated version, Parallel
Eureka, which applies LLMs-driven reward functions with-
out the final enhancement step. This isolates the impact of
preference-based learning on reward quality and effective-
ness. The results reveal  that AED outperforms or matches
Parallel Eureka in nearly all settings, and shows especially
large gains when the original LLMs-driven rewards are only
partially effective. For instance, in the Highway environment
with a D3QN-driven tested policy and three adversarial ve-
hicle agents, AED improves the effective Left Lane Change
vulnerability rate improves significantly from 32.22% with
Parallel Eureka to 88.89%. This improvement highlights the
impact of preference-based enhancement  in guiding adver-
sarial vehicles to consistently orchestrate failure scenarios
that  the tested policy struggles to handle. Additionally,  in
cases where Parallel Eureka already performed well, AED
either maintains or improves performance, indicating that the
preference-based refinement enhances reward fidelity without

These results confirm that AED is  the most  effective
method for both uncovering hidden vulnerabilities  in the
tested driving policy and exploiting those weaknesses to trig-
ger consistent  failures. The preference-based enhancement
plays a crucial role in converting suboptimal LLMs-driven
reward signals into high-precision objectives for adversarial
policy training.
D.  Interpretability and Discussion
To illustrate  the functionality of  the AED framework,
we present a case study in Fig. 4,  identifying Left Lane
Change vulnerability in the Highway environment with a
D3QN-driven  tested policy and  three adversarial  vehicle
agents.

First, we prompt the LLMs with relevant environment
context and task description, including the targeted accident
type (Left Lane Change Collision), a textual description of
traffic scenarios where the tested policy executes a left lane
change maneuver that results in a collision, and responsibility
criteria to determine the tested policy’s fault.
Based on these descriptions, the LLM-generated K (In our
experiment, K was set to be 6) executable reward functions
for Left Lane Change Collision and trained K corresponding
agents  in the environment using these reward functions.
Next, we use these agents and the policy under evaluation
to generate accident videos  in the environment. Then,  a
human evaluator selects the best reward function based on
the proportion of effective left-lane-change collisions caused
by the agents in the videos.
The chosen reward function  is presented  in  the mid-
dle of Fig. 4. We can observe that  the generated reward
function contains key components to ensure the function’s
effectiveness. It first verifies that the tested AV’s maneuver
prior  to  the collision was  indeed a Left Lane Change.
To prevent aggressive adversarial behavior,  it also includes
constraints that limit the adversarial vehicles’ steering angles.

We propose AED, an LLM-based framework that uses
reinforcement  learning to automatically discover effective
and diverse vulnerabilities of autonomous driving policies.
We use an LLM to automatically design reward functions
without the need of human experts. To enhance the diversity
of vulnerabilities, we use the LLM the consider a broad range
of potential accidents and train adversarial policies using RL
in parallel. We further  improve the effectiveness of each
vulnerability by training a reward model with preference-
based learning to filter ineffective accidents and other types
of accidents. Experiments on various driving scenarios with
both planning-based policies  and  learning-based policies
show that AED discovers a diverse range of vulnerabilities
and archives higher attack success rate compared to existing
methods, which shows the potential of using LLMs to reduce
the need for manual  reward engineering and improve the
comprehensiveness of autonomous driving safety evaluation.
